prompt_md:
Your company runs an AI tool that helps make **customer-facing, regulated lending decisions**. It was reviewed and approved once at launch and entered in the AI use register at **high tier**. Since then the vendor has shipped a **new model version** and your team has begun feeding it **new applicant data fields**, all without any re-review.

You can make **one** governance improvement next. Given that the risk here comes from a high-tier use **drifting after a one-time approval**, which change best addresses the root cause — and why?

decision_choices: Negotiate a lower price with the AI vendor to reduce program cost
Make governance continuous: add re-review triggers on any model-version or data-field change, schedule recurring bias testing, and keep the register and risk tier current
Switch to a model with a larger context window so it can read more applicant history
Remove the human-in-the-loop step to speed up loan decisions
reason_choices: Because the exposure is drift after a one-time gate — the model, data, and law change over time, so only continuous re-tiering, bias testing, and an up-to-date register keep the controls proportionate to the real risk
Because a cheaper contract reduces the bias and explainability risk of automated lending
Because a larger context window guarantees fair and explainable lending decisions
Because removing human oversight makes the audit trail stronger
